chunk_summary: Guidelines for India-specific nutritional substitutions in chronic kidney and liver disease when specialized formulas are unavailable, focusing on energy density, protein adequacy, and micronutrient safety.
learning_objectives: Understand substitution strategies for renal and hepatic nutrition when disease-specific formulas are unavailable.
Learn methods to increase energy density and protein adequacy without excess phosphate or free water.
Recognize the importance of monitoring for essential fatty acid deficiency (EFAD) and vitamin absorption in cholestasis.
Develop practical feeding plans for children with fluid restrictions or cholestasis.
key_facts: Breast milk or standard infant formula is prioritized for pediatric renal patients, with energy density increased using carbohydrates or fats.
In older children, calorie boosters like oil or ghee can be added to home foods, with reassessment every 2–4 weeks.
Coconut oil is not a full substitute for MCT oil in cholestasis; essential fatty acid exposure must be ensured.
Signs of EFAD include dry, scaly dermatitis and poor growth, especially when functional fats dominate calorie intake.
Water-miscible fat-soluble vitamins are preferred in cholestasis; standard formulations have unpredictable absorption.
reference_quote: The dietary management of energy and protein in children with CKD stages 2–5 and on KDOQI Clinical Practice... (Reference 4)
document_key_topics: Substitution strategies for unavailable renal or hepatic formulas
Energy density and protein adequacy in pediatric nutrition
Monitoring for essential fatty acid deficiency and vitamin absorption
